notable.notable_blurb: Father of New Haven's park system, historian

narrative_blocks.header: Father of New Haven's park system
narrative_blocks.detail: "As far back as 1887 he foresaw the need of a modern and extended park system for the city," wrote Rev. Oscar Maurer at Blake's 1922 memorial. "Twenty-five years ago he was known as the Father of the New Haven park system, and it really was an appellation that belonged to him." He watched the boys and girls at Beaver Ponds and mapped it out for public use; he insisted on the expansion of East Rock; he foresaw Fort Hale as a park. He was elected honorary life member of the New Haven Chamber of Commerce in 1913 in recognition of this work. (HathiTrust Memorial Address 1922)
narrative_blocks.body: Appointed to the Commission on Public Parks in 1889, Blake served as president from 1898 to 1911. Nine parks totaling 650+ acres opened during his tenure. East Rock Park was expanded at his insistence; after his death, a portion was named Blake Field in his honor.

narrative_blocks.detail: Rev. Maurer described Blake's writing as "marked by accuracy, well-balanced judgment, wit of a truly Attic quality, and a whimsical humor which smiles at one from every page." Blake closed the Chronicles with a challenge still quoted in New Haven: "May it be guarded from encroachment in the future more jealously than in the past; and may our successors in its care of every race and lineage protect its soil, and cherish its traditions with that affectionate veneration which is the heritage and the test of every true son and daughter of New Haven." (Blake, Chronicles of New Haven Green, 1898)
narrative_blocks.body: Blake's 1898 history of the Green, drawn from papers read before the New Haven Colony Historical Society, became the definitive account of the city's public square from 1638 to 1862. He served as the Society's secretary for twenty years.

narrative_blocks.header: Yale 1848 — believed the oldest living Yale
narrative_blocks.detail: He was clerk of the Supreme and Superior Courts for Fairfield County (1868–1884), gave the principal address at New Haven's 250th anniversary in 1888, helped select and erect the Defenders' Monument, and was on Abraham Lincoln's Bridgeport reception committee in 1860. His mother was a great-granddaughter of Rev. James Pierpont (Yale co-founder), making his Yale connection an inheritance as well as an achievement. (HathiTrust Memorial Address 1922; Yale Biographical Sketches)
narrative_blocks.body: Blake graduated Yale in 1848 and was admitted to the bar in 1850. He practiced in Bridgeport as Treat & Blake until 1885, then returned to New Haven. He died April 6, 1922 — eleven days before his 94th birthday — believed to be the oldest Yale alumnus in New England.

narrative_blocks.detail: In later visits, Maurer found Blake "alert on the subject and keen to know what further contributions modern scholarship has made. He spoke modestly of his work — as always almost deprecatingly — said that it was a side issue, something done to satisfy his own mind on the subject." The same restless intelligence drove his park advocacy, his historical writing, and his long legal career. (HathiTrust Memorial Address 1922)
narrative_blocks.body: In 1866 Blake published The Rise and Fall (Hurd & Houghton), anticipating modern interpretations of the opening chapters of Genesis — years before the debate became mainstream in American churches. Rev. Maurer called it "certainly the first to be written by a layman" on the subject in America.

cross_connections.display_label: Wrote the definitive biography of Yale president Ezra Stiles
Yale president Timothy Dwight called him the 'American Addison'

narrative_blocks.detail: Born in Windham, Connecticut in 1778, Kingsley graduated Yale in 1799 and became a tutor there in 1801. Appointed to the professorship of Hebrew, Greek, and Latin in 1805, he was elected Fellow of the American Academy of Arts and Sciences in 1825. His publications included critical editions of Tacitus and Cicero's De Oratore, a history of Yale College (American Quarterly Register, 1835), and a biography of Ezra Stiles — Yale's seventh president — for Jared Sparks's Library of American Biography. He resigned in 1851 and died in New Haven on August 31, 1852. His daughter Elizabeth Coit Kingsley married Henry Taylor Blake (HD1568), connecting him to the Hooker tree. His portrait, painted in 1828 by Nathaniel Jocelyn, hangs at the Yale University Art Gallery. (Wikipedia; Yale Biographical Sketches)
narrative_blocks.body: James Luce Kingsley taught Latin, Greek, and Hebrew at Yale for nearly five decades (1805–1851). Yale president Timothy Dwight called him 'the American Addison'; Yale president Woolsey later said he doubted any American scholar had ever surpassed Kingsley in Latin style.

Kingsley co-authored the Yale Report of 1828 — the defense of classical education that shaped American colleges for half a century — and wrote the Ezra Stiles biography for Sparks’s American Biography. Stiles, like the family Kingsley’s daughter married into, descended from Hooker.

career.role: Professor of Hebrew, Greek, and Latin

INST022.primary_name: American Board of Commissioners for Foreign Missions
INST022.name: American Board of Commissioners for Foreign Missions
INST022.notes: First American foreign-mission organization. Tree connections: Rev. Elias Cornelius (agent 1816, secretary 1831-32); Ainsworth and Harriet Ellsworth Blunt, missionaries to the Cherokee at Brainerd, Tennessee; Eliza Talcott, missionary to Japan; Rev. William Banfield Capron, Madura mission; Judge John Hooker, member.
